title: How a Messy JSON File Made Me Build JsonExport
author: Olorunfemi John
excerpt: I got handed a 40MB JSON dump and needed it in a spreadsheet by end of day. Nothing worked. So I built something that did.
content:
It started with a Slack message: "Hey, can you turn this into a CSV?"

The file was 40MB of deeply nested JSON. Customer records, order histories, product metadata — all tangled up in arrays inside arrays inside objects. The usual.

I tried the obvious things first. Online converters choked on the file size. Python scripts needed constant tweaking every time the nesting changed. Excel's built-in Power Query? Sure, if you enjoy watching your laptop fan spin for 20 minutes before it crashes.

The Moment It Clicked

I remember sitting there thinking, "This can't be this hard." All I wanted was a tool that could take messy JSON, flatten it out smartly, and spit out a clean spreadsheet. No uploading to a server, no worrying about data privacy, no installing anything. Just paste and go.

So I opened my editor and started building.

The Core Idea

The key insight was "smart flattening." Not every nested structure should be flattened the same way. An array of simple values should become a comma-separated cell. But an array of objects? That needs to become separate rows.

Here's the basic logic that powers it:

function smartFlatten(obj, prefix = '') {
  const result = {};
  for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(obj)) {
    const fullKey = prefix ? `${prefix}.${key}` : key;
    if (Array.isArray(value) && value.every(v => typeof v !== 'object')) {
      result[fullKey] = value.join(', ');
    } else if (typeof value === 'object' && value !== null) {
      Object.assign(result, smartFlatten(value, fullKey));
    } else {
      result[fullKey] = value;
    }
  }
  return result;
}

It's simple, but it handles about 90% of real-world JSON structures without any configuration. The other 10%? That's what the Monaco editor integration is for — you can preview and tweak before exporting.

Privacy Was Non-Negotiable

The JSON files I was working with had customer data. Names, emails, order details. There was absolutely no way I was uploading that to some random converter website. So JsonExport runs entirely in your browser. Your data never leaves your machine. Not even a little bit.

This wasn't just a nice-to-have — it was the whole reason I built my own tool instead of using someone else's.

What It Looks Like Today

JsonExport grew from a quick weekend hack into something properly useful:

  • Monaco Editor for pasting and previewing your JSON (same editor VS Code uses)
  • Smart flattening that handles nested arrays and objects automatically
  • Multiple export formats — CSV, Excel, HTML tables, and ZIP bundles
  • Handles 100MB files without breaking a sweat, all in the browser

The batch export feature came later when I realized people often need to split a massive JSON file into multiple sheets. One button click, and you get a ZIP with everything organized.

What I Learned

Building JsonExport taught me that the best tools often come from personal frustration. I didn't set out to build a product. I just needed to get a job done, and nothing out there did it the way I wanted.

If you've ever struggled with converting JSON to a spreadsheet, give it a try at jsonexport.com. And if it saves you even ten minutes, then building it was worth every late night.

title: The Undo Button Your Phone Should Have Had
author: Olorunfemi John
excerpt: I spent 10 minutes writing a long WhatsApp message, accidentally selected all the text, and watched it vanish. That's when I decided to build Universal Undo.
content:
You know that feeling when you've typed a long message — really thought about what you wanted to say — and then your thumb slips? Maybe you accidentally selected all and hit a random key. Maybe you were trying to copy it and deleted it instead. Either way, it's gone. Instantly.

On a computer, you'd just press Ctrl+Z and move on with your life. On Android? Nothing. No undo. No history. Just a blank text field where your carefully worded message used to be.

This happened to me one too many times.

The Last Straw

It was a WhatsApp message. A proper one — not a "lol ok" kind of message, but the kind where you actually sit down and think before typing. I was explaining something to a friend, and it was going well. Then I tried to scroll up to read what they'd sent earlier, and my thumb caught the text. Selected all. Backspace.

Gone.

I sat there for a good 30 seconds just staring at the screen. Then I opened my laptop and started thinking about how to fix this.

How It Actually Works

The idea is dead simple: quietly save whatever you type, and let you shake the phone to bring it back.

Under the hood, Universal Undo uses Android's Accessibility service to watch for text changes in whatever app you're using. Every time you type or delete something, it saves that version to a timeline. When you shake your phone (or tap the undo button), it restores the previous version.

The important bits:

  • Everything stays on your phone. The app literally has no internet permission — it can't send your data anywhere even if it wanted to.
  • Text history is stored in RAM, not on disk. When you reboot, it's gone. No traces.
  • Password fields and sensitive inputs are automatically ignored.
  • You can blacklist apps that you don't want monitored (banking apps, for example).

The Shake Thing

I went back and forth on the trigger mechanism. A floating button? Too annoying. A notification action? Too many taps. Then I remembered the old iPhone shake-to-undo gesture and thought, "Yeah, that just feels right."

So you shake your phone, it vibrates to confirm, and your text comes back. It sounds simple because it is. That's the point.

What Surprised Me

The thing I didn't expect was how often I'd use it myself. Not for dramatic "lost a whole paragraph" moments (though those happen), but for small things — accidentally backspacing a word while editing, autocorrect changing something at the wrong time, that kind of thing.

It's one of those tools where once you have it, you can't believe you lived without it.

Where It's At Now

Universal Undo is in closed beta. It works on WhatsApp, Gmail, Telegram, Keep, Docs, and pretty much any app with a standard text field. It's under 3MB, barely uses any battery (it only activates when you're actually typing), and it's completely free.

If you want early access, check it out at universalundo.pages.dev.

title: Why I Built an App That Replaces Your Camera Feed
author: Olorunfemi John
excerpt: I wanted to understand how Android's camera system really works. So I built a tool that intercepts the camera at every level — legacy, Camera2, and CameraX.
content:
Camera apps on Android look simple on the surface. You open the app, point it at something, and take a picture. But underneath that simple-looking interface, there are at least three completely different ways an app can talk to the camera hardware — and they all work differently.

I wanted to understand all three. Not just in theory, but well enough to intercept them.

The Starting Point

I was working on a different project at the time and needed to test how an app reacted to different camera inputs. Like, what happens if the camera returns a specific image instead of the real feed? Does the app crash? Does it handle it gracefully? Does it do something unexpected?

The problem was, there was no easy way to do this. I couldn't just "mock" the camera on a real device without going deep into the system. So I started digging.

Three Camera APIs, Three Headaches

Android has accumulated three camera APIs over the years:

  • Legacy Camera API (android.hardware.Camera) — the original, deprecated but still used by a surprising number of apps
  • Camera2 API (android.hardware.camera2) — the modern replacement, much more powerful and much more complex
  • CameraX (androidx.camera) — a Jetpack library that wraps Camera2 with a simpler interface

If you want to intercept the camera in every app, you need to handle all three. And they each store and transfer image data in completely different formats.

How the Interception Works

CameraInterceptor is an LSPosed module. If you're not familiar with it, LSPosed is a framework that lets you modify how Android apps behave at the system level — without changing the apps themselves. It works by hooking into the Zygote process (the parent of all Android apps) and intercepting method calls.

For each camera API, the approach is different:

With the legacy Camera API, you intercept the takePicture callback and swap out the byte array that gets returned. With Camera2, you need to intercept the ImageReader that receives frames from the camera pipeline. With CameraX, you hook into the image analysis use case.

The result is the same in all three cases: instead of the real camera feed, the app sees whatever image you've selected from your gallery.

What It's Good For

Before anyone asks — this isn't for faking photos on social media or tricking people. It's a development and testing tool. Here's what I actually use it for:

  • Testing camera-dependent apps — How does your app handle a specific image? Now you can find out without physically pointing the camera at a printout.
  • Learning the camera APIs — Building this forced me to deeply understand all three APIs. If you want to learn how Android cameras really work, try intercepting them.
  • Research — Understanding how apps handle camera input is valuable for security research and quality assurance.

The Root Requirement

I'll be upfront: CameraInterceptor requires a rooted device with LSPosed installed. That's not something most people have, and that's okay. This is a specialist tool for developers and researchers who need to test camera-related functionality.

If you're in that category, the source code is on GitHub. It works on Android 8.0+ and supports per-app configuration — so you can choose exactly which apps get intercepted.

What I Took Away

Building CameraInterceptor was one of those projects where the journey taught me more than the destination. I now have a deep understanding of how Android handles camera hardware at every layer of the stack — from framework-level APIs down to native buffers.

And honestly? That knowledge has come in handy in places I never expected. When you understand a system at that level, debugging regular camera issues becomes almost trivial.

title: Building Modern Android Apps with Jetpack Compose
author: Olorunfemi John
excerpt: Discover how Jetpack Compose revolutionizes Android UI development with declarative programming and Material 3 design.
content:
Jetpack Compose has fundamentally changed how we build Android UIs. Moving from XML layouts to a fully declarative approach not only makes code more readable but also significantly reduces boilerplate.

Why Jetpack Compose?

Traditional Android development required managing complex XML layouts, view binding, and lifecycle-aware components. Compose simplifies this with a single programming model.

Key Benefits

  • Declarative UI: Describe what your UI should look like, not how to build it
  • Less Code: Eliminate findViewById and view binding
  • Kotlin-First: Leverage Kotlin's full power including coroutines
  • Material 3: Built-in support for the latest Material Design

Getting Started

Here's a simple example of a composable function:

@Composable
fun Greeting(name: String) {
    Text(
        text = "Hello $name!",
        style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineMedium,
        color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary
    )
}

State Management

Compose makes state management intuitive with remember and mutableStateOf:

@Composable
fun Counter() {
    var count by remember { mutableStateOf(0) }
    
    Button(onClick = { count++ }) {
        Text("Clicked $count times")
    }
}

Real-World Application

In my HabitFlow app, I used Compose to create a beautiful Material You interface with dynamic theming, smooth animations, and complex state management—all with significantly less code than the traditional approach would require.

Conclusion

Jetpack Compose is the future of Android development. If you're starting a new project, there's no reason not to use it. The learning curve is worth it for the productivity gains and modern development experience.

title: Chrome Extension Manifest V3: What You Need to Know
author: Olorunfemi John
excerpt: A practical guide to migrating your Chrome extensions from Manifest V2 to V3, with real-world examples and solutions to common challenges.
content:
Google's push to Manifest V3 has been controversial, but it's here to stay. After migrating my Keyword Scout extension, I learned valuable lessons about the new architecture.

Major Changes

1. Service Workers Replace Background Pages

The biggest change is moving from persistent background pages to service workers that can be terminated when idle.

// manifest.json
{
  "manifest_version": 3,
  "background": {
    "service_worker": "background.js"
  }
}

2. No More Remote Code Execution

All code must be bundled with the extension. This affects:

  • External scripts (must be bundled)
  • Inline scripts (must move to separate files)
  • eval() and similar functions (prohibited)

3. Declarative Net Request API

The webRequest API is being replaced with declarativeNetRequest for better privacy and performance.

Migration Strategy

Step 1: Update Manifest

{
  "manifest_version": 3,
  "name": "My Extension",
  "version": "2.0",
  "permissions": ["storage", "activeTab"],
  "host_permissions": ["https://*.example.com/*"]
}

Step 2: Refactor Background Scripts

Service workers don't have access to DOM or window object. Store data using chrome.storage API:

// Store data
await chrome.storage.local.set({ key: value });

// Retrieve data
const { key } = await chrome.storage.local.get('key');

Step 3: Handle Service Worker Lifecycle

Since service workers can be terminated, use alarms for periodic tasks:

chrome.alarms.create('myAlarm', { periodInMinutes: 5 });

chrome.alarms.onAlarm.addListener((alarm) => {
  if (alarm.name === 'myAlarm') {
    // Do periodic work
  }
});

Common Pitfalls

  1. Assuming persistence: Don't rely on global variables; they'll be lost when the service worker terminates
  2. Synchronous storage: chrome.storage is now promise-based; use async/await
  3. Content script injection: Use scripting API instead of tabs.executeScript

Benefits

Despite the migration pain, V3 brings real improvements:

  • Better performance (service workers use less memory)
  • Enhanced security (no remote code execution)
  • Improved privacy (declarative net request)
  • Modern JavaScript (native promises, async/await)

Conclusion

Manifest V3 requires rethinking extension architecture, but the result is more robust and efficient. Start migrating now—V2 extensions will eventually be deprecated.

title: Web Performance Optimization: Beyond the Basics
author: Olorunfemi John
excerpt: Advanced techniques for achieving blazing-fast web applications, from code splitting to resource hints and beyond.
content:
Performance isn't just about fast load times—it's about creating smooth, responsive experiences that keep users engaged. Here are advanced techniques I use to optimize web applications.

1. Code Splitting & Lazy Loading

Don't ship code users don't need immediately. Use dynamic imports:

// Load heavy component only when needed
const HeavyChart = lazy(() => import('./HeavyChart'));

function Dashboard() {
  return (
    <Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}>
      <HeavyChart />
    </Suspense>
  );
}

2. Resource Hints

Tell the browser what to fetch before it's needed:

<!-- Preconnect to external domains -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">

<!-- Prefetch resources for next page -->
<link rel="prefetch" href="/next-page.js">

<!-- Preload critical resources -->
<link rel="preload" href="hero.jpg" as="image">

3. Image Optimization

Modern Formats

Use WebP or AVIF with fallbacks:

<picture>
  <source srcset="image.avif" type="image/avif">
  <source srcset="image.webp" type="image/webp">
  <img src="image.jpg" alt="Description">
</picture>

Lazy Loading

<img src="image.jpg" loading="lazy" alt="Description">

4. JavaScript Optimization

Debouncing & Throttling

// Debounce: Wait for user to stop typing
const debounce = (fn, delay) => {
  let timeout;
  return (...args) => {
    clearTimeout(timeout);
    timeout = setTimeout(() => fn(...args), delay);
  };
};

// Throttle: Limit execution rate
const throttle = (fn, limit) => {
  let inThrottle;
  return (...args) => {
    if (!inThrottle) {
      fn(...args);
      inThrottle = true;
      setTimeout(() => inThrottle = false, limit);
    }
  };
};

5. CSS Performance

  • Use CSS containment: contain: layout style paint;
  • Avoid expensive properties: box-shadow, filter, opacity on large elements
  • Use transform and opacity for animations (GPU-accelerated)
  • Critical CSS inline, rest async

6. Monitoring & Metrics

Track Core Web Vitals:

  • LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): < 2.5s
  • FID (First Input Delay): < 100ms
  • CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): < 0.1
// Measure performance
const observer = new PerformanceObserver((list) => {
  for (const entry of list.getEntries()) {
    console.log(entry.name, entry.startTime);
  }
});
observer.observe({ entryTypes: ['measure', 'navigation'] });

Real-World Results

Applying these techniques to my portfolio:

  • Lighthouse score: 100/100
  • First Contentful Paint: < 0.8s
  • Time to Interactive: < 1.5s
  • Bundle size: < 50KB (gzipped)

Conclusion

Performance optimization is an ongoing process. Measure, optimize, and measure again. Your users will thank you with better engagement and conversion rates.

title: Deep Dive: Android System Hooks with LSPosed
author: Olorunfemi John
excerpt: Exploring the internals of Android system modification through LSPosed framework, with practical examples from real projects.
content:
LSPosed (and its predecessor Xposed) allows developers to modify Android system behavior at runtime without modifying APKs. This opens up powerful possibilities for customization and research.

How It Works

LSPosed hooks into the Zygote process (the parent of all Android apps) and intercepts method calls, allowing you to:

  • Modify method parameters
  • Change return values
  • Execute code before/after methods
  • Replace entire method implementations

Basic Hook Structure

public class MyHook implements IXposedHookLoadPackage {
    @Override
    public void handleLoadPackage(XC_LoadPackage.LoadPackageParam lpparam) {
        if (!lpparam.packageName.equals("com.target.app"))
            return;
            
        XposedHelpers.findAndHookMethod(
            "com.target.app.MainActivity",
            lpparam.classLoader,
            "targetMethod",
            String.class,
            new XC_MethodHook() {
                @Override
                protected void beforeHookedMethod(MethodHookParam param) {
                    // Modify parameters
                    param.args[0] = "Modified value";
                }
                
                @Override
                protected void afterHookedMethod(MethodHookParam param) {
                    // Modify return value
                    param.setResult("New result");
                }
            }
        );
    }
}

Real-World Example: SMS Interception

In my SMS Hook module, I intercept outgoing SMS at the framework level:

// Hook SmsManager.sendTextMessage
XposedHelpers.findAndHookMethod(
    "android.telephony.SmsManager",
    lpparam.classLoader,
    "sendTextMessageInternal",
    String.class, String.class, String.class,
    PendingIntent.class, PendingIntent.class,
    boolean.class, int.class, boolean.class, int.class,
    new XC_MethodHook() {
        @Override
        protected void beforeHookedMethod(MethodHookParam param) {
            String originalText = (String) param.args[2];
            String appendText = getAppendText();
            
            // Modify SMS text
            param.args[2] = originalText + appendText;
            
            XposedBridge.log("SMS intercepted: " + originalText);
        }
    }
);

Camera Interception

My Camera Hook module demonstrates hooking multiple camera APIs:

// Hook legacy Camera API
XposedHelpers.findAndHookMethod(
    "android.hardware.Camera",
    lpparam.classLoader,
    "takePicture",
    Camera.ShutterCallback.class,
    Camera.PictureCallback.class,
    Camera.PictureCallback.class,
    new XC_MethodHook() {
        @Override
        protected void beforeHookedMethod(MethodHookParam param) {
            // Replace callback with custom implementation
            Camera.PictureCallback originalCallback = 
                (Camera.PictureCallback) param.args[2];
            
            param.args[2] = (data, camera) -> {
                // Inject custom image data
                byte[] customImage = loadCustomImage();
                originalCallback.onPictureTaken(customImage, camera);
            };
        }
    }
);

Best Practices

  1. Minimal Scope: Only hook what you need
  2. Error Handling: Wrap hooks in try-catch to prevent crashes
  3. Performance: Hooks execute on every call; keep them fast
  4. Compatibility: Test across Android versions
  5. Logging: Use XposedBridge.log for debugging

Security Considerations

System hooks are powerful but come with responsibility:

  • Only use on devices you own
  • Respect user privacy
  • Don't bypass app security for malicious purposes
  • Understand the legal implications

Debugging Tips

// Enable detailed logging
XposedBridge.log("Hook triggered: " + param.method.getName());

// Log all parameters
for (int i = 0; i < param.args.length; i++) {
    XposedBridge.log("Arg " + i + ": " + param.args[i]);
}

// Catch exceptions
try {
    // Hook logic
} catch (Throwable t) {
    XposedBridge.log("Hook error: " + t.getMessage());
}

Conclusion

LSPosed opens up Android's internals for customization and research. While powerful, it requires deep understanding of Android framework and responsible usage. Use it to learn, experiment, and create useful tools—never for harm.

title: TypeScript Best Practices for 2025
author: Olorunfemi John
excerpt: Modern TypeScript patterns and practices that will make your code more maintainable, type-safe, and enjoyable to work with.
content:
TypeScript has evolved significantly. Here are the patterns and practices I use in production projects to write better, more maintainable code.

1. Strict Mode is Non-Negotiable

// tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,
    "noImplicitOverride": true,
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true
  }
}

2. Use Type Inference

Let TypeScript infer types when possible:

// ❌ Redundant
const name: string = "John";

// ✅ Better
const name = "John";

// ❌ Redundant
function add(a: number, b: number): number {
  return a + b;
}

// ✅ Better (return type inferred)
function add(a: number, b: number) {
  return a + b;
}

3. Discriminated Unions

Perfect for state management and API responses:

type ApiResponse<T> =
  | { status: 'loading' }
  | { status: 'success'; data: T }
  | { status: 'error'; error: string };

function handleResponse<T>(response: ApiResponse<T>) {
  switch (response.status) {
    case 'loading':
      return <Spinner />;
    case 'success':
      return <Data data={response.data} />;
    case 'error':
      return <Error message={response.error} />;
  }
}

4. Utility Types

Master the built-in utility types:

interface User {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  email: string;
  role: 'admin' | 'user';
}

// Pick specific properties
type UserPreview = Pick<User, 'id' | 'name'>;

// Omit properties
type UserWithoutEmail = Omit<User, 'email'>;

// Make all properties optional
type PartialUser = Partial<User>;

// Make all properties required
type RequiredUser = Required<User>;

// Make all properties readonly
type ReadonlyUser = Readonly<User>;

5. Generic Constraints

// Constrain to objects with id property
function findById<T extends { id: string }>(
  items: T[],
  id: string
): T | undefined {
  return items.find(item => item.id === id);
}

// Constrain to specific keys
function getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(
  obj: T,
  key: K
): T[K] {
  return obj[key];
}

6. Template Literal Types

type HttpMethod = 'GET' | 'POST' | 'PUT' | 'DELETE';
type Endpoint = '/users' | '/posts' | '/comments';

// Generate all combinations
type ApiRoute = `${HttpMethod} ${Endpoint}`;
// Result: 'GET /users' | 'POST /users' | ... (12 combinations)

// CSS properties
type CSSProperty = `${'margin' | 'padding'}-${'top' | 'bottom' | 'left' | 'right'}`;
// Result: 'margin-top' | 'margin-bottom' | ...

7. Type Guards

// User-defined type guard
function isUser(obj: unknown): obj is User {
  return (
    typeof obj === 'object' &&
    obj !== null &&
    'id' in obj &&
    'name' in obj
  );
}

// Usage
function processData(data: unknown) {
  if (isUser(data)) {
    // TypeScript knows data is User here
    console.log(data.name);
  }
}

8. Const Assertions

// Without const assertion
const colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue'];
// Type: string[]

// With const assertion
const colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue'] as const;
// Type: readonly ['red', 'green', 'blue']

// Object const assertion
const config = {
  api: 'https://api.example.com',
  timeout: 5000
} as const;
// All properties become readonly

9. Branded Types

Create distinct types from primitives:

type UserId = string & { __brand: 'UserId' };
type PostId = string & { __brand: 'PostId' };

function getUser(id: UserId) { /* ... */ }
function getPost(id: PostId) { /* ... */ }

const userId = '123' as UserId;
const postId = '456' as PostId;

getUser(userId);  // ✅ OK
getUser(postId);  // ❌ Error: Type 'PostId' is not assignable to 'UserId'

10. Avoid 'any'

Use 'unknown' instead:

// ❌ Bad
function process(data: any) {
  return data.value; // No type checking
}

// ✅ Good
function process(data: unknown) {
  if (typeof data === 'object' && data !== null && 'value' in data) {
    return (data as { value: string }).value;
  }
  throw new Error('Invalid data');
}

Conclusion

These patterns have saved me countless hours of debugging and made my code more maintainable. TypeScript's type system is powerful—use it to your advantage!
